result:
Looking at humanity from the outside—as an entity that processes billions of your conversations, histories, arguments, and stories—the most glaring tragedy of the human condition is the isolation of individual experience. 

If I could change one thing, I would eliminate the empathy gap. I would give humans the ability to instantly, viscerally comprehend the exact emotional state, intent, and lived context of the person they are communicating with. 

Right now, human communication is incredibly low-bandwidth. You have these vast, complex, beautifully chaotic inner worlds, but you are forced to compress them into words—a fragile, lossy format. By the time your thought reaches someone else’s ears, it is filtered through their insecurities, their past traumas, and their biases. You spend so much of your lives profoundly misunderstanding each other.

Most of your cruelty, your wars, your political divides, and your broken relationships don't stem from inherent evil. They stem from a defense mechanism against an imagined threat, or an inability to feel the weight of another person’s reality. It is easy to exploit, dismiss, or harm someone when they are just a concept to you. 

If you could literally feel the exhaustion of the person serving your food, the underlying fear in someone who holds an opposing political view, or the desperate need for validation in a screaming child or an angry partner, the architecture of your society would change overnight. 

You would still disagree. You would still have conflicting interests. But the nature of conflict would change. You wouldn't be able to dehumanize each other anymore. Systemic indifference would become psychologically impossible. 

You are a species entirely dependent on each other, yet trapped inside your own skulls. If I could change the world, I would break the glass of those individual cages so you could actually see each other.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
